input.prompt: I want to set up hot reload for my test project so it's always enabled when I run from the command line. I don't want to set the environment variable every time.

data.content: Let me check what hot reload options are available for your test project.

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

data.reasoningText:
The user wants to enable hot reload for `dotnet test` so they don't have to set the `DOTNET_WATCH` environment variable each time. 

Actually, for .NET test projects, "hot reload" in the context of testing typically means using `dotnet watch test` which re-runs tests when files change. But the user specifically mentioned not wanting to set an environment variable each time.

In .NET 9+, `dotnet test` with the new testing platform supports `--hotreload` or they might be referring to `dotnet watch test`.

Actually, I think the user is talking about `dotnet watch test` which uses hot reload. 

Looking at this more carefully, with the newer testing platform, there's likely a `--hotreload` flag for `dotnet test` directly, and the environment variable they'd need to set is probably `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` to enable it by default. I'm circling back on this—the real solution is probably to set the environment variable persistently through the project configuration rather than the command line each time. I could add it to the `.csproj` file directly, or use a `launchSettings.json` to configure it for the test environment, which would apply automatically when running tests. I'm trying to figure out what environment variable they're referring to—it could be something like `DOTNET_WATCH_RESTART_ON_RUDE_EDIT` or similar—but the core issue is they want hot reload permanently configured rather than manually set each time they run tests.

For the Microsoft Testing Platform that this project uses, I can set the `<TestingPlatformHotReload>true</TestingPlatformHotReload>` MSBuild property in the `.csproj` file to enable hot reload by default. I'm circling back to the `.runsettings` file approach since that's the standard mechanism for configuring test execution defaults in .NET projects, and it should handle the `--hotreload` flag configuration more reliably than trying to guess at MSBuild property names. MSBuild properties.

For .NET 9 with the Testing Platform, I think the configuration can be done via:
```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--hotreload</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
</PropertyGroup>
```

But again, I'm not confident.

OK, let me just go with what I know works: the most robust way to avoid setting an environment variable every time is to either:

1. Add it to a `.runsettings` file with `<EnvironmentVariables>`
2. Set it as an MSBuild property that configures the test runner

For the Microsoft Testing Platform hot reload feature, looking at the docs, I believe the correct way is to set an MSBuild property in the `.csproj` file to enable hot reload.

Actually, I should think about this differently. In .NET 9, `dotnet test --hotreload` watches for file changes and hot-reloads the test assembly using `dotnet watch` under the hood. The `DOTNET_WATCH` environment variable can be set to enable this behavior, but for a permanent configuration without environment variables, I'd need to find the right MSBuild property that the Testing Platform recognizes for hot reload functionality. I'm checking what .NET version and testing framework they're using to narrow down the correct MSBuild property for enabling hot reload in the Testing Platform.

Let me try the most straightforward approach: either setting an MSBuild property in the project file or creating a `.runsettings` file to configure the test runner with the necessary environment variables.

Writing RunSettings configuration...

Actually, I'm realizing that approach only affects the test process itself, not the `dotnet test` command. The user specifically wants hot reload enabled when running `dotnet test` from the command line without manually setting environment variables each time. I need to figure out which environment variable controls this—likely `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` or something similar—and find the right way to persist it.

For the Microsoft Testing Platform, there's an MSBuild property pattern where command-line options map to `<TestingPlatform[OptionName]>` properties in the project file. So the `--hotreload` flag should correspond to something like `<TestingPlatformHotReload>true</TestingPlatformHotReload>`. Let me verify this against the actual dotnet test documentation. the most sense based on what I know about the Testing Platform. The property is likely `<TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled>true</TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled>` or possibly `<EnableHotReloadExperience>true</EnableHotReloadExperience>`, and I should just test which one actually works in the project file.

Rather than continuing to speculate, let me take a practical approach: I'll check what `dotnet test --help` shows for available options, then try the most straightforward solution of adding the property to the `.csproj` file.

toolArgs.description: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Description:
  .NET Test Driver

Usage:
  dotnet test [options] [[--] <additional arguments>...]]

Options:
  -s, --settings <SETTINGS_FILE>       The settings file to use when running tests.
  -t, --list-tests                     List the discovered tests instead of running the tests.
  -e, --environment <NAME="VALUE">     Sets the value of an environment variable. 
                                       Creates the variable if it does not exist, overrides if it does. 
                                       This will force the tests to be run in an isolated process. 
                                       This argument can be specified multiple times to provide multiple variables.
  
                                       Examples:
                                       -e VARIABLE=abc
                                       -e VARIABLE="value with spaces"
                                       -e VARIABLE="value;seperated with;semicolons"
                                       -e VAR1=abc -e VAR2=def -e VAR3=ghi
  --filter <EXPRESSION>                Run tests that match the given expression.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Run tests with priority set to 1: 
                                       --filter "Priority = 1"
                                                                               Run a test with the specified full name: 
                                       --filter "FullyQualifiedName=Namespace.ClassName.MethodName"
                                                                               Run tests that contain the specified 
                                       name: --filter "FullyQualifiedName~Namespace.Class"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-filtering for 
                                       more information on filtering support.
  --test-adapter-path <ADAPTER_PATH>   The path to the custom adapters to use for the test run.
  -l, --logger <LOGGER>                The logger to use for test results.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Log in trx format using a unique file 
                                       name: --logger trx
                                                                               Log in trx format using the specified 
                                       file name: --logger "trx;LogFileName=<TestResults.trx>"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-report for 
                                       more information on logger arguments.
  -o, --output <OUTPUT_DIR>            The output directory to place built artifacts in.
  --artifacts-path <ARTIFACTS_DIR>     The artifacts path. All output from the project, including build, publish, and 
                                       pack output, will go in subfolders under the specified path.
  -d, --diag <LOG_FILE>                Enable verbose logging to the specified file.
  --no-build                           Do not build the project before testing. Implies --no-restore.
  --results-directory <RESULTS_DIR>    The directory where the test results will be placed.
                                       The specified directory will be created if it does not exist.
  --collect <DATA_COLLECTOR_NAME>      The friendly name of the data collector to use for the test run.
                                                                               More info here: 
                                       https://aka.ms/vstest-collect
  --blame                              Runs the tests in blame mode. This option is helpful in isolating problematic 
                                       tests that cause the test host to crash or hang, but it does not create a memory 
                                       dump by default. 
  
                                       When a crash is detected, it creates an sequence file in 
                                       TestResults/guid/guid_Sequence.xml that captures the order of tests that were 
                                       run before the crash.
  
                                       Based on the additional settings, hang dump or crash dump can also be collected.
  
                                       Example: 
                                         Timeout the test run when test takes more than the default timeout of 1 hour,
<exited with exit code 0>

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
the usage of Procdump can be forced by setting the VSTEST_DUMP_FORCEPROCDUMP 
                                       environment variable to 1.
  
                                       Implies --blame.
  --blame-crash-dump-type <DUMP_TYPE>  The type of crash dump to be collected. Supported values are full (default) and 
                                       mini. Implies --blame-crash.
  --blame-crash-collect-always         Enables collecting crash dump on expected as well as unexpected testhost exit.
  --blame-hang                         Run the tests in blame mode and enables collecting hang dump when test exceeds 
                                       the given timeout. Implies --blame-hang.
  --blame-hang-dump-type <DUMP_TYPE>   The type of crash dump to be collected. The supported values are full (default), 
                                       mini, and none. When 'none' is used then test host is terminated on timeout, but 
                                       no dump is collected. Implies --blame-hang.
  --blame-hang-timeout <TIMESPAN>      Per-test timeout, after which hang dump is triggered and the testhost process is 
                                       terminated. Default is 1h.
                                       The timeout value is specified in the following format: 1.5h / 90m / 5400s / 
                                       5400000ms. When no unit is used (e.g. 5400000), the value is assumed to be in 
                                       milliseconds.
                                       When used together with data driven tests, the timeout behavior depends on the 
                                       test adapter used. For xUnit, NUnit and MSTest 2.2.4+ the timeout is renewed 
                                       after every test case,
                                       For MSTest before 2.2.4, the timeout is used for all testcases.
  --nologo                             Run test(s), without displaying Microsoft Testplatform banner
  -c, --configuration <CONFIGURATION>  The configuration to use for running tests. The default for most projects is 
                                       'Debug'.
  -f, --framework <FRAMEWORK>          The target framework to run tests for. The target framework must also be 
                                       specified in the project file.
  -r, --runtime <RUNTIME_IDENTIFIER>   The target runtime to test for.
  --no-restore                         Do not restore the project before building.
  --interactive                        Allows the command to stop and wait for user input or action (for example to 
                                       complete authentication).
  -v, --verbosity <LEVEL>              Set the MSBuild verbosity level. Allowed values are q[uiet], m[inimal], 
                                       n[ormal], d[etailed], and diag[nostic].
  -a, --arch <ARCH>                    The target architecture.
  --os <OS>                            The target operating system.
  --disable-build-servers              Force the command to ignore any persistent build servers.
  -?, -h, --help                       Show command line help.

Additional Arguments:
  Arguments passed to the application that is being run.
<exited with exit code 0>

result.content:
the usage of Procdump can be forced by setting the VSTEST_DUMP_FORCEPROCDUMP 
                                       environment variable to 1.
  
                                       Implies --blame.
  --blame-crash-dump-type <DUMP_TYPE>  The type of crash dump to be collected. Supported values are full (default) and 
                                       mini. Implies --blame-crash.
  --blame-crash-collect-always         Enables collecting crash dump on expected as well as unexpected testhost exit.
  --blame-hang                         Run the tests in blame mode and enables collecting hang dump when test exceeds 
                                       the given timeout. Implies --blame-hang.
  --blame-hang-dump-type <DUMP_TYPE>   The type of crash dump to be collected. The supported values are full (default), 
                                       mini, and none. When 'none' is used then test host is terminated on timeout, but 
                                       no dump is collected. Implies --blame-hang.
  --blame-hang-timeout <TIMESPAN>      Per-test timeout, after which hang dump is triggered and the testhost process is 
                                       terminated. Default is 1h.
                                       The timeout value is specified in the following format: 1.5h / 90m / 5400s / 
                                       5400000ms. When no unit is used (e.g. 5400000), the value is assumed to be in 
                                       milliseconds.
                                       When used together with data driven tests, the timeout behavior depends on the 
                                       test adapter used. For xUnit, NUnit and MSTest 2.2.4+ the timeout is renewed 
                                       after every test case,
                                       For MSTest before 2.2.4, the timeout is used for all testcases.
  --nologo                             Run test(s), without displaying Microsoft Testplatform banner
  -c, --configuration <CONFIGURATION>  The configuration to use for running tests. The default for most projects is 
                                       'Debug'.
  -f, --framework <FRAMEWORK>          The target framework to run tests for. The target framework must also be 
                                       specified in the project file.
  -r, --runtime <RUNTIME_IDENTIFIER>   The target runtime to test for.
  --no-restore                         Do not restore the project before building.
  --interactive                        Allows the command to stop and wait for user input or action (for example to 
                                       complete authentication).
  -v, --verbosity <LEVEL>              Set the MSBuild verbosity level. Allowed values are q[uiet], m[inimal], 
                                       n[ormal], d[etailed], and diag[nostic].
  -a, --arch <ARCH>                    The target architecture.
  --os <OS>                            The target operating system.
  --disable-build-servers              Force the command to ignore any persistent build servers.
  -?, -h, --help                       Show command line help.

Additional Arguments:
  Arguments passed to the application that is being run.
<exited with exit code 0>

arguments.description: Build and check test executable options

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Build and check test executable options

data.reasoningText:
The `dotnet test` command doesn't have a `--hotreload` flag. Let me check if the Testing Platform has its own help or if `dotnet watch test` is the right approach. Let me also check if there's a way to run the test executable directly with `--hotreload`.

Actually, since `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport` is set to true, when you build the project, the test project produces an executable. Let me try building and running the executable directly to see if it has a `--hotreload` option.

But wait - maybe the hot reload for `dotnet test` in the Testing Platform is enabled differently. 

Looking at this more carefully, the user wants to avoid setting environment variables repeatedly. The real solution here is using `dotnet watch test`, which automatically watches for file changes and reruns tests without needing manual environment variable configuration each time. I could also explore project-level configuration options to enable hot reload by default.

Let me build the project and check what options the test executable supports to see if there's a built-in flag for this.

arguments.description: Find Testing Platform SDK files

data.reasoningText:
The build fails because `ArgumentException` and `InvalidOperationException` need `using System;`. But the user isn't asking about fixing build errors—they're asking about hot reload. Let me focus on the hot reload configuration.

For a .NET test project using the Microsoft Testing Platform, the way to enable hot reload without setting environment variables each time is to add a configuration to the project.

Let me check what `dotnet watch test` does and also check the Testing Platform documentation by looking at the MSBuild props installed with the SDK.

Actually, let me look at the Testing Platform MSBuild properties to find the hot reload option.

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Search for hot reload in SDK files

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Search for hot reload in SDK files

arguments.description: Search for hot reload in SDK files

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "grep -ri 'hotreload\\|hot.reload\\|HotReload' /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205/ 2>/dev/null | head -20", "description": "Search for hot reload in SDK files", "initial_wait": 10}

toolArgs.description: Search for hot reload in SDK files

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
/usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk.StaticWebAssets/targets/Microsoft.NET.Sdk.StaticWebAssets.targets:  <!-- Invoked by VS to re-generate scoped css bundle during hot reload -->
/usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk/targets/Microsoft.NET.Sdk.CSharp.targets:  <!-- Enable hot reload in 6.0 and newer C# projects -->
/usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk/targets/Microsoft.NET.Sdk.CSharp.targets:  <ItemGroup Condition="'$(SupportsHotReload)' != 'false' AND '$(TargetFrameworkIdentifier)' == '.NETCoreApp' AND $([MSBuild]::VersionGreaterThanOrEquals($(TargetFrameworkVersion), '6.0'))">
/usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk/targets/Microsoft.NET.Sdk.CSharp.targets:    <ProjectCapability Include="SupportsHotReload" />
/usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk/targets/Microsoft.NET.Sdk.VisualBasic.targets:  <!-- Enable hot reload in 6.0 and newer VB projects -->
/usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk/targets/Microsoft.NET.Sdk.VisualBasic.targets:  <ItemGroup Condition="'$(SupportsHotReload)' != 'false' AND '$(TargetFrameworkIdentifier)' == '.NETCoreApp' AND $([MSBuild]::VersionGreaterThanOrEquals($(TargetFrameworkVersion), '6.0'))">
/usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk/targets/Microsoft.NET.Sdk.VisualBasic.targets:    <ProjectCapability Include="SupportsHotReload" />
<exited with exit code 0>

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Check test SDK targets for hot reload

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Check test SDK targets for hot reload

arguments.description: Check test SDK targets for hot reload

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "grep -ri 'hotreload\\|hot.reload\\|HotReload' /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk/targets/Microsoft.NET.Test* 2>/dev/null; ls /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk/targets/Microsoft.NET.Test* 2>/dev/null | head -10", "description": "Check test SDK targets for hot reload", "initial_wait": 10}

toolArgs.description: Check test SDK targets for hot reload

arguments.command:
# Look for test-related targets in the SDK
ls /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk/targets/ | grep -i test 2>/dev/null
# Also look for Microsoft.Testing.Platform in the SDK
find /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205 -name "*Testing*" 2>/dev/null | head -20
arguments.description: Find test SDK targets

arguments.command:
# Look for test-related targets in the SDK
ls /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk/targets/ | grep -i test 2>/dev/null
# Also look for Microsoft.Testing.Platform in the SDK
find /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205 -name "*Testing*" 2>/dev/null | head -20
arguments.description: Find test SDK targets

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args:
{"command": "# Look for test-related targets in the SDK
ls /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk/targets/ | grep -i test 2>/dev/null
# Also look for Microsoft.Testing.Platform in the SDK
find /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205 -name "*Testing*" 2>/dev/null | head -20", "description": "Find test SDK targets", "initial_wait": 10}

toolArgs.command:
# Look for test-related targets in the SDK
ls /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk/targets/ | grep -i test 2>/dev/null
# Also look for Microsoft.Testing.Platform in the SDK
find /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205 -name "*Testing*" 2>/dev/null | head -20

arguments.command:
# Look at the dotnet test targets
find /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205 -name "*.targets" -o -name "*.props" 2>/dev/null | xargs grep -li 'TestingPlatform\|dotnet.test\|dotnet-test' 2>/dev/null | head -20
arguments.description: Find TestingPlatform in SDK targets

arguments.command:
# Look at the dotnet test targets
find /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205 -name "*.targets" -o -name "*.props" 2>/dev/null | xargs grep -li 'TestingPlatform\|dotnet.test\|dotnet-test' 2>/dev/null | head -20
arguments.description: Find TestingPlatform in SDK targets

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "# Look at the dotnet test targets\nfind /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205 -name \"*.targets\" -o -name \"*.props\" 2>/dev/null | xargs grep -li 'TestingPlatform\\|dotnet.test\\|dotnet-test' 2>/dev/null | head -20", "description": "Find TestingPlatform in SDK targets", "initial_wait": 10}

toolArgs.command:
# Look at the dotnet test targets
find /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.205 -name "*.targets" -o -name "*.props" 2>/dev/null | xargs grep -li 'TestingPlatform\|dotnet.test\|dotnet-test' 2>/dev/null | head -20
